title: Cat Profuse Bleeding from Ear Canal: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: Profuse bleeding from a cat's ear canal is a high-priority emergency. Learn the causes and why immediate veterinary care is essential for your pet.
content_html:
TL;DR: Profuse ear bleeding in cats is a high-priority emergency that requires immediate veterinary attention to identify the source of blood loss and prevent life-threatening complications.

What does profuse ear bleeding in my cat look like?

Profuse bleeding from the ear canal in cats involves active, heavy blood flow originating from the internal structures of the ear. This is distinct from minor surface scratches and often indicates a significant underlying medical issue that requires professional intervention.

Is profuse ear bleeding in cats considered a medical emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. Active or profuse bleeding from the ear is considered a veterinary emergency.
  • This condition can lead to rapid blood loss, severe pain, or indicate life-threatening issues such as head trauma, ruptured tumors, or systemic clotting disorders.
  • You should transport your cat to the nearest emergency veterinary hospital immediately.

How can taking a photo of my cat's ear help the triage team?

  • Capturing a clear photograph of the ear and the surrounding blood allows the medical staff to assess the volume and source of the blood—whether it is coming from the ear flap or deep within the canal—before you even arrive.
  • This information helps the hospital prioritize your cat's care and prepare the appropriate diagnostic tools.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cases of profuse bleeding from the ear canal in cats, emergency triage should consider potential causes such as ear canal tumors, especially in middle-aged to older cats, or nasopharyngeal polyps, more common in young cats. Clinical signs associated with ear canal tumors include unilateral chronic otic discharge (ceruminous, purulent, mucoid, or hemorrhagic), necrotic odor, head shaking, and ear scratching. Nasopharyngeal polyps, benign inflammatory growths, can also cause purulent discharge. Middle or inner ear involvement may manifest as neurologic signs, including deafness, vestibular signs (head tilt, ataxia, nystagmus), facial nerve palsy or paralysis (facial drooping, salivation, dropping food from the lips), Horner syndrome (eyelid droop, pinpoint pupil, and inward rolling of the globe), and occasional protrusion of the third eyelid. Radiography, CT, or MRI may be helpful in identifying masses or other abnormalities.

Chapter: Surgery, Emergency, General Principles

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2009)

faqs.question: Should I try to clean the blood out of the ear?
faqs.answer: No, do not attempt to clean deep inside the ear canal or insert cotton swabs, as this can worsen the injury or push a foreign object deeper. Gently wipe only the outer fur if necessary for transport.

faqs.question: What are common causes of ear hemorrhage in cats?
faqs.answer: Common causes include traumatic injury, ruptured inflammatory polyps, severe chronic infections, ear canal tumors (carcinomas), or blood clotting disorders.

faqs.question: How can I safely transport my bleeding cat?
faqs.answer: Place your cat in a secure, dark carrier lined with soft towels. Try to keep the cat calm and minimize head shaking, as this can increase the bleeding.
